description: The device exposes up to 32 GPIO signals grouped as two banks of 16 pins each. Banked organization determines bit positions across all control and status registers. This structure simplifies uniform per-pin operations via shared 32-bit bank-pair registers. It enables scalable access patterns for firmware manipulating multiple pins. The mapping of pins to banks and bits is explicitly defined.
evidence: Section 2.2 Signal Descriptions; Section 2.5 GPIO Register Structure; Table 1
dependencies: PSC clock enable; Pin multiplexing configuration
Remarks: Use bank and bit mappings to compute register masks efficiently; verify package-specific availability per device data manual.

description: Each pin can be independently configured as input or output via the DIR01 register. A value of 1 configures the pin as input and 0 as output, with all pins defaulting to inputs after hardware reset. Direction affects whether output write registers have effect on a pin. This allows mixed input/output usage within the same bank. Proper direction setup is foundational to safe GPIO operations.
Remarks: Initialize DIR01 before driving outputs; confirm defaults after hardware reset to avoid unintended drive.

feature_name: Atomic output set/clear and direct write
description: Output state can be changed using dedicated SET_DATA01 and CLR_DATA01 registers or by direct writes to OUT_DATA01. Set/clear registers allow atomic bit manipulation without read-modify-write sequences. This supports concurrent software contexts controlling different pins safely. OUT_DATA01 provides full-port write semantics when required. Readbacks of these registers reflect programmed outputs, not necessarily the external pin level if configured as input.
evidence: Section 1.2 Features; Section 3.4 OUT_DATA01; Section 3.5 SET_DATA01; Section 3.6 CLR_DATA01
Remarks: Prefer SET/CLR for concurrency and to avoid race conditions; use OUT_DATA01 only when full-port updates are intended.

feature_name: Separate input and output readback paths
description: The peripheral provides distinct input and output status visibility. IN_DATA01 returns the synchronized external pin state, while OUT_DATA01 returns the drive value for output-configured pins. This separation aids debugging of board-level electrical issues versus software drive intent. It also enables reading wired-OR/open-drain combined states when applicable. Accurate interpretation requires using IN_DATA01 for actual logic level checks.
evidence: Section 1.2 Features; Section 2.6.2; Section 3.4 OUT_DATA01; Section 3.7 IN_DATA01
Remarks: Always sample IN_DATA01 for true pin state, especially with external pull-ups or when pins use open-drain drivers as noted in the device data manual.

description: Each GPIO can generate interrupts on rising, falling, both, or neither edge. Edge detection is synchronized to the GPIO clock to ensure robust event capture. Configuration uses SET_RIS_TRIG01/CLR_RIS_TRIG01 and SET_FAL_TRIG01/CLR_FAL_TRIG01 registers. Interrupts can be generated even for pins configured as outputs to aid debug. Status and clearing are managed via INTSTAT01 with write-one-to-clear semantics.
evidence: Section 2.10.3; Section 3.8–3.11 (SET_RIS_TRIG01, CLR_RIS_TRIG01, SET_FAL_TRIG01, CLR_FAL_TRIG01); Section 3.12 INTSTAT01
dependencies: BINTEN bank enable; CPU interrupt mapping (device data manual)
Remarks: Debounce or filter edges externally or in software; ensure both-edge configurations are justified to control interrupt rates.

description: Interrupt/event generation is enabled per bank of 16 pins using BINTEN. EN0 controls Bank 0 and EN1 controls Bank 1. These bits gate both DSP interrupt requests and EDMA events from the GPIO. Proper bank enables are required in addition to per-pin edge configurations. This provides coarse-grain control to disable all events from a bank quickly.
dependencies: CPU interrupt controller setup; EDMA event routing (device data manual)
Remarks: Enable only the banks in active use to reduce spurious events; document which pins in each bank are armed.

description: Pending GPIO interrupts are indicated per pin and cleared by writing a 1 to the corresponding bit. The register provides a consolidated view for both banks within the bank-pair interface. Software must service all pending sources to prevent missed edges. For directly routed CPU GPIO interrupts, CPU flags may also reflect status as noted. Coordinated clearing ensures consistent state between CPU and peripheral.
Remarks: Use read-modify-write patterns that write back only the pending bits to clear; avoid clearing unrelated events.

description: GPIO can generate synchronization events for the EDMA to trigger transfers. This enables low-latency reactions to external signals without CPU polling. BINTEN bits are noted as controlling both interrupt and EDMA event paths. Exact event numbers and channel mappings are device-specific and must be obtained from the data manual. This feature supports efficient GPIO-to-memory logging and timestamping workflows.
evidence: Section 1.2 Features; Section 2.10.1; Section 2.11; Section 3.2 note
dependencies: EDMA event and channel mapping (device data manual); BINTEN configured
Remarks: Design EDMA transfers to be idempotent and rate-limited; use edge selection to bound trigger frequency.

feature_name: Clock control via PSC (CPU/6 peripheral clock)
description: The GPIO peripheral clock is disabled by default and must be enabled through the local Power and Sleep Controller. Once enabled, the GPIO clock runs at CPU/6. Register access and edge synchronization depend on this clock being active. Correct clock control is a prerequisite for initialization and operation. Disabling the clock through PSC halts peripheral activity until re-enabled.
dependencies: PSC module configuration (DSP Subsystem User's Guide)
Remarks: Ensure PSC enable completes before touching GPIO registers; consider CPU/6 timing when budgeting interrupt latency.

description: GPIO supports reduced-power modes managed by the PSC. While in low power, interrupt generation is suspended even if edges occur. Output pins maintain their driven states across low-power entry and exit. Proper sequencing is required when restoring from low power to re-arm interrupts. This behavior allows power saving without disturbing external interfaces.
dependencies: PSC power policy; System low-power framework
Remarks: On resume, re-enable BINTEN and reconfigure edge triggers if needed; verify no critical edges were missed during suspend.

description: GPIO pins are part of an extensive pin multiplexing scheme on the device. Hardware configuration at reset and software-programmable registers determine whether a package pin functions as GPIO or another peripheral. Correct mux selection is mandatory before GPIO operation. Conflicts with other subsystems must be resolved per board design. Refer to the device data manual for the exact mux controls and defaults.
dependencies: Device pinmux registers; Board design constraints
Remarks: Apply pinmux early in boot before enabling GPIO edges; document mux ownership to avoid runtime contention.

description: Software resets such as emulator-initiated resets do not modify GPIO configuration or pin states. PSC-invoked resets that disable the GPIO clock and assert PSC reset restore default configuration settings. Hardware resets return GPIO configuration and data registers to their reset states. Understanding these behaviors is essential for predictable system reset recovery. Plan initialization accordingly after any reset source.
Remarks: After hardware or PSC reset, reapply pinmux, clock enable, directions, and interrupt configurations in order.

description: A defined sequence is provided to bring the GPIO module up after hardware reset. Steps include configuring pin multiplexing, enabling the module via PSC, and programming direction, data, and interrupt control registers. Following this order ensures registers are accessible and pins are safe. It prevents driving unintended states on shared pins. Successful completion readies the module for data transactions.
dependencies: Pinmux configured; PSC enabled; DIR/INT registers programmed
Remarks: Implement initialization as an idempotent routine callable after any reset or low-power exit.

description: Input sampling is synchronized to the GPIO peripheral clock using internal flip-flops. This reduces metastability risk and provides clean edge detection. As a result, IN_DATA01 reflects synchronized values rather than raw asynchronous levels. Edge-trigger logic similarly operates in the clock domain. External debouncing may still be required for noisy signals.
evidence: Section 2.7.2; Figure 1 Functional Block Diagram (Synchronizing flip-flops)
Remarks: Account for one or more clock cycles of synchronization latency when timing very short pulses.

description: GPIO operation and register semantics are independent of endianness. The same firmware logic applies for both big-endian and little-endian CPU modes. This simplifies portable driver implementations across system configurations. Bit positions and field meanings are unaffected. No special handling is required in the GPIO layer.
Remarks: Keep byte-lane considerations in higher-level memory operations; GPIO register access itself is unaffected.

description: The GPIO peripheral is not affected by emulation suspend events such as halts and breakpoints. GPIO continues to operate and can generate interrupts while the CPU is halted. This can aid hardware validation but may require caution during debug. Developers should manage breakpoints to avoid unintended live I/O changes. Awareness prevents surprises when inspecting I/O during halted states.
Remarks: Disable or mask GPIO events during critical debug sessions if external side effects are undesirable.

description: GPIO interrupt sources are not multiplexed with other interrupt functions on this device. This simplifies routing and identification of events in the interrupt controller. It reduces ambiguity when debugging ISR sources. Software mapping still depends on device-specific interrupt numbers. Clear isolation supports deterministic interrupt handling.
dependencies: CPU interrupt vector mapping (device data manual)
Remarks: Document the exact interrupt numbers used for GPIO banks or pins per system integration.

description: A read-only PID register provides peripheral identification including function code and revision. It enables software to detect the GPIO IP version at runtime. This is useful for applying conditional workarounds or logging system configuration. The function value for GPIO is specified along with major and minor revisions. Consistent identification aids maintainability across silicon revisions.
Remarks: Verify PID at init and assert expected values (FUNCTION=0x483, MAJOR=0x1, MINOR=0x5) to catch mismatched silicon early.
